subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Parametric Particle Motion
stimulus: For time $t \geq 0$, a particle moves in the $xy$-plane with position $(x(t), y(t))$ and velocity vector $\left(\sin(t^2), e^{\cos t}\right)$. At time $t = 0$, the particle is at position $(1, 5)$.

parts.prompt: Find the speed of the particle at time $t = 1.2$. Find the acceleration vector of the particle at time $t = 1.2$.

scoring_points.description: Speed at $t = 1.2$: $\text{speed} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\sin^2(1.44) + e^{2\cos(1.2)}} \approx 1.491$ (or equivalent calculator answer)

Acceleration vector at $t = 1.2$: $\mathbf{a}(1.2) = \left(2t\cos(t^2), -\sin(t)\cdot e^{\cos t}\right)\big|_{t=1.2} \approx (2(1.2)\cos(1.44),\, -\sin(1.2)\cdot e^{\cos(1.2)}) \approx (0.355, -1.049)$ (or equivalent calculator answer)

parts.prompt: Find the total distance traveled by the particle over the time interval $0 \leq t \leq 1.2$.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral setup for arc length (total distance): $\int_0^{1.2} \sqrt{\left(\sin(t^2)\right)^2 + \left(e^{\cos t}\right)^2}\,dt$
Correct numerical answer using a calculator: total distance $\approx 1.443$ (or equivalent)

parts.prompt: Find the coordinates of the point at which the particle is farthest to the left for $t \geq 0$. Explain why there is no point at which the particle is farthest to the right for $t \geq 0$.

scoring_points.description: Sets $\frac{dx}{dt} = \sin(t^2) = 0$ and identifies a critical point where $x$ is minimized, e.g., $t^2 = \pi$ so $t = \sqrt{\pi}$

Justifies that the particle is farthest left at $t = \sqrt{\pi}$ (e.g., $\sin(t^2) < 0$ for $0 < t < \sqrt{\pi}$ so $x$ is decreasing, then $\sin(t^2) > 0$ so $x$ is increasing — or by sign analysis of $dx/dt$)

required_elements: Sign analysis showing $\frac{dx}{dt}$ changes from negative to positive at $t = \sqrt{\pi}$

scoring_points.description: $y$-coordinate at $t = \sqrt{\pi}$: $y(\sqrt{\pi}) = 5 + \int_0^{\sqrt{\pi}} e^{\cos t}\,dt \approx 5 + 3.848 \approx 8.848$ (calculator value); states the leftmost point coordinates

Explanation for no farthest-right point: since $e^{\cos t} > 0$ for all $t$, we have $\frac{dy}{dt} > 0$ always, so $y(t) \to \infty$. Also, $\frac{dx}{dt} = \sin(t^2)$ alternates sign infinitely often and $x(t)$ increases without bound (the positive portions of $\sin(t^2)$ outweigh the negative portions over time), so $x(t) \to \infty$ and there is no farthest right point.

required_elements: As $t \to \infty$, $x(t) \to \infty$ so no farthest-right point exists
